clean_story:
Invisible waves. Voices without wires.

The word radio comes from radius, Latin for "spray" or "beam"—fitting, since it sprays invisible signals through the air like a hose sprays water.

Radio starts with electricity vibrating at precise speeds, creating waves that zip through space at light’s speed. A transmitter (like a tower’s antenna) pushes these waves outward; a receiver (your car radio) catches them and turns them back into sound. No cables. No delay. Early radios used spark gaps—literally snapping electricity between metal to make crude signals. By 1906, Reginald Fessenden broadcast the first voice and music (a violin solo) on Christmas Eve, proving radio could carry more than dots and dashes.

Today, AM radio bounces signals off the atmosphere to travel hundreds of miles at night; FM stays local but resists static. Emergency beacons, GPS, and even Wi-Fi all descend from radio’s core trick: encoding information in waves. The Apollo moon landings? Radio carried every word and heartbeat 238,855 miles home.

Three related subjects are sound as energy, how antennas shape signals, and light as a wave.
